column1: What could your college or university do to address food and housing insecurity? Please share a solution(s).
column6: They are doing a great job.
CNM does a food pantry. Expanding and increasing how often it offers food as well as the types of food couldn't hurt.
provide nutritious low cost meals on campus

I really don't know. It seems asking for free stuff just makes for people to take advantage that causes those that really need it to go without. Someone has to pay for the freebies. But it seems the usual recipients are the scammers
More availability around campus and provide information about food distribution options.
provide housing vouchers
I am fine.
On campus housing
low/moderate cost meal options for students and staff. Actively participate in conversations regarding the lack or minimal amount of affordable safe housing in Albuquerque.
Affordable healthy food during school hours

Provide more food options and events with free food in multiple locations and also do a better job advertising. I often see events with free food for faculty and sometimes staff throughout the day for different occasions, but rarely for students. If anything, events with free food for students tend to be at nighttime, or that's what it feels like.
Greater food bank drives/availability
Allow people to take care of themselves and do not provide any type of assistance.
Pay employees a living wage.  I have a Ph.D. and make $15.50 an hour.
Have food pantries on all of the campuses - if addressing the need reveals this would help
We have a food pantry that I think is well received at Main Campus.  Adding one to South Valley, Montoya, and West Side would help.
No suggestions
Farmer .or growers market on campus that accept good programs
Increase locations of food pantries on CNM campuses. Increase student and staff awareness of sources that could promote or provide housing security.
Increase our wages to keep up with inflation rates. Provide affordable healthy balanced meals on campus all the time during the week.
Provide day care on campus that is excellent quality and safe and affordable.
Support community gardens, not have torn down the UNM student housing complex that could have housed many homeless.
Bring more food trucks to the campus to serve the population, and with more affordable options and more locations around campus.
more days open for the CNM Main Campus food panrty.  It's only open once a week, and the line is always wrapped around the building.  Students and some employees need more!
provide better, more affordable options for food on all its campuses
Offer affordable AND healthy food options. Build dorms for students with families. Or offer affordable housing for students near campus.
I feel that my school does a lot to help students with food insecurities.

More affordable on campus food options.
Publicize the food pantry
Food vouchers, utilities vouchers.

more food at the food pantry

I teach one class at a charter high school in the south valley. I take food there regularly because they don't "like" the food the school serves and always complain they are hungry, so they gladly take my packaged food which is primarily junk food. But I figure a treat is a treat, and if they know someone cares, that could mean a lot to them. The systems aren't working for everyone. I'm so lucky to be in such a place of privilege that I didn't earn. I lead a very privileged life compared to the students at the charter school. For example:
There was a shooting a few weeks ago. Two students were killed. Another student misses her dad who left her a decade ago to go to Mexico once he was "wanted" and has come back to the area but hasn't responded that he wants to see her. Another student lost a 2-year-old cousin about 2 weeks ago. Most all live in single parent homes. Apartments. South Valley houses with extended families sometimes. These kids have a very hard life, right here in Albuquerque where we ought to be able to do better. 
I share this because I can see the disparity and it breaks my heart. 
Thanks for initiating this survey to see what needs are out there.

This food insecurity issue has been blown WAY out of proportion- In fact---I often see morbidly obese and just overweight and fat students all around campus- and I am not alone- when I lunch with co-workers we see obesity and students eating plates and plates of pizza- instead of a nutritious well balanced meal

There is a lot of need among our students.  I have made down-payments for students to secure an apartment, given students money for food, to pay a phone bill, etc.  I have found that my male students experiencing food, house insecurity, or homelessness have less support than other students.

Some students have indicated that the meal plan they pay for is problematic because they can't use it due to closing hours (many of them work) or that they don't find the food options worth what they pay. This leads them to pay for a meal plan because they are required to do so and still seek other food options, which are often less healthy since they can't cook in the dorms.
In glancing around the cafeteria and the town--one sees many many many morbidly obese and moderately overweight individuals- rarely does one see thin people in this community---

The city of Albuquerque has decreased bus services; they discontinued a route that would place me within walking distance of my home. I now must ride later bus and walk in the dark home at lease 1-2 times a week. The commuter routes feel safer than the Lomas route (example) I have had two scary rides with people who had outbursts and were threatening to the bus driver and other passengers. the city bus is much more focused on down-town workers with regular work hours and not UNM workers who have later and earlier working hours that are not consistent. I  have only one car and share with my spouse so that I can afford for my disabled son to have transportation; I pay for the least expensive UNM parking, but park in the lot only about once a week. It is expensive parking for just once a week.
UNMH does not have a program or set protocol for adult individuals that have traumatic brain injury. It is very difficult to get services and their is not a set protocol as there is in other states. This is a shame as the incidence of TBI is high and it is so very difficult to get SSDI for this condition. As the flagship University and hub for medical care in NM this it is a shame that we do not have a set protocol for caring with individuals who sustain TBI.

Could this model be extended to universities to support the consumption of healthy foods by student populations? In addition to paying service providers to run fast food restaurants on campus, could a portion of the budget be used to support local farmers and subsidize the cost of fresh produce on campuses?
This is a multidimensionsl, complex issue. This survey is a great start.

column6: Our campus also needs a mental health center for students who are dealing with trauma, stress, etc. We have too many students who are struggling without any support. And referring them to a community clinic or UNM main campus is not as effective as having a mental health clinic on-campus.

Adjunct professors are under appreciated and underpaid. Increase salary is critical.

When I first started as an Adjunct instructor I wasn't allowed to teach more than 12hrs a week, so my take home salary was very low the first year. So low, I had to live with my parents the first year. Then when we hired the new instructor, he got Full-Time Employee pay right from the beginning, when I had to struggle my first year working for UNM. It was really unfair.

There were signs that they had previously painted over mold before I moved in, and it gradually grew back through the walls and ceilings. I now have several severe chronic illnesses from living in that apartment for 3 years, but can't afford a lawyer. I had to live out of my car for some time to escape the black mold. DOH didn't respond to my complaint about the property, that I know of.

I was in HUD housing for seniors and disabled. My next-door neighbor rapped on the wall when he could hear that I was in the bedroom at night and in the middle of the night sometimes. I was always on edge because I never knew when this would happen.  I wrote him a note asking him to stop and it got worse for the next two weeks. I felt like I had to tiptoe around the bedroom and bathroom area which had a shared wall with him.  The other next-door neighbor has an alcohol and deafness problem and is diabetic.  I had to call wellness check on him one day because no one could get ahold of him. Thankfully he was alive, but this was very disturbing to me. He also played music loud in the morning.
